stem: Let \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[d1]] & [[u12]] & [[u13]] \\ 0 & [[d2]] & [[u23]] \\ 0 & 0 & [[d3]] \end{bmatrix} \). The determinant of a matrix equals the product of its eigenvalues. What is \( \det(A) \)?
solution: The matrix is upper triangular, so its eigenvalues are the diagonal entries \( [[d1]], [[d2]], [[d3]] \). The determinant is their product \( ([[d1]])([[d2]])([[d3]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This adds the eigenvalues (the trace) instead of multiplying them.
This multiplies only two of the three eigenvalues.
This multiplies only two of the three eigenvalues.
This multiplies only two of the three eigenvalues.
This is one more than the correct determinant.
This is one less than the correct determinant.
This is two more than the correct determinant.
This is two less than the correct determinant.

stem: A \( 3 \times 3 \) matrix \( A \) has eigenvalues \( [[l1]] \), \( [[l2]] \), and \( [[l3]] \). What is \( \det(A) \)?
solution: The determinant of a matrix always equals the product of its eigenvalues: \( \det(A) = [[l1]]\cdot[[l2]]\cdot[[l3]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This adds the eigenvalues (giving the trace) instead of multiplying them.
This multiplies only two of the three eigenvalues.
This multiplies only two of the three eigenvalues.
This mixes addition and multiplication instead of multiplying all three.
This is one more than the correct determinant.
This is one less than the correct determinant.
This is three more than the correct determinant.

stem: Let \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[d]] & [[u12]] & [[u13]] \\ 0 & [[d]] & [[u23]] \\ 0 & 0 & [[d3]] \end{bmatrix} \), which has a repeated eigenvalue. Recall that \( \det(A) \) equals the product of ALL eigenvalues, counted with multiplicity. What is \( \det(A) \)?
solution: The matrix is upper triangular with diagonal entries \( [[d]], [[d]], [[d3]] \), so its eigenvalue \( [[d]] \) has multiplicity \( 2 \). Counting multiplicity, \( \det(A) = ([[d]])([[d]])([[d3]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This multiplies the repeated eigenvalue only once instead of twice (its multiplicity).
This adds the eigenvalues (with the repeat counted) instead of multiplying them.
This ignores the third, distinct eigenvalue entirely.
This adds instead of multiplies, and drops the repeated factor.
This is one more than the correct determinant.
This is one less than the correct determinant.
This is two more than the correct determinant.

stem: Let \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] \\ [[c]] & k \end{bmatrix} \). Since \( \det(A) \) is the product of \( A \)'s eigenvalues, \( A \) is singular exactly when \( \det(A) = 0 \). For what value of \( k \) is \( A \) singular?
distractor_rationales: This has the wrong sign on the solution for \( k \).
This solves for the wrong entry, swapping the roles of \( b \) and \( c/a \).
This forgets to divide by \( a \) when isolating \( k \).
This ignores \( b \) and \( c \) entirely and just negates \( a \).
This is one more than the correct value of \( k \).
This is one less than the correct value of \( k \).

stem: Let \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[d1]] & [[u12]] & [[u13]] \\ 0 & [[d2]] & [[u23]] \\ 0 & 0 & [[d3]] \end{bmatrix} \), which is invertible since all its eigenvalues are nonzero. Since \( \det(A^{-1}) \) equals the product of the reciprocals of the eigenvalues of \( A \), what is \( \det(A^{-1}) \)?
solution: The eigenvalues of \( A \) are \( [[d1]], [[d2]], [[d3]] \), so \( \det(A) = [[d1]][[d2]][[d3]] \) and \( \det(A^{-1}) = \dfrac{1}{\det(A)} = \dfrac{1}{[[d1]]\cdot[[d2]]\cdot[[d3]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This gives \( \det(A) \) itself instead of \( \det(A^{-1}) \), forgetting to take the reciprocal.
This has the correct reciprocal but the wrong sign.
This adds the reciprocals of the eigenvalues instead of multiplying them.
This is the trace of \( A \), not \( \det(A^{-1}) \).
This is one more than the correct value.
This is one less than the correct value.
